text:
In her 50s, after being a stay at home mom, a few years ago my mother in law started an online pillow store. 

It makes over $1,000,000 a year and very profitable. 

She didn’t know anything about computers. Came to America in her teens, learned English, raised a family then said she still had a fire in her…so she started a business. 

She knew nothing about the internet. 

She only works on it a few days a week as she babysits her grandkids. 

I did a podcast on my first million with her. 

I’m honor we are family!

Her story is so inspiring. 

Tech bro. Stay at home mom. Ambitious college kid. Trust me. Listen to it. It will change you.

text:
In ~2012 @noahkagan and @nevmed made a janky YouTube video on copywriting and business. 

It changed my life. I wanted to start an internet company and be a copywriter. 

I loved how they lived and talked and acted. I wanted to be their friend! So I cold emailed them. 

Now 14 years later, I had an internet company, know copywriting, and we are family+our wives and kids are close. 

These guys helped me in business, helped me mature, and inspired me more than anyone!

Very lucky to have this friendship!

text:
Most people have no idea how pretty the lobby of this building is.

Its a bank on the Upper West Side (near my house).

Funny story: Its prolly worth $100-150m.

Roughly 5-10% of the entire bank's value (just the building...(these numbers are guesses).

It was built in 1928. Modeled after an Italian Renaissance palazzo. Lobby has 65-foot ceilings. Taller than most churches.

Its one of the few grand banking halls still operating as an actual bank. 

Most people walk past it every day and have no idea what's inside.

If you're ever on the Upper West Side, walk in. It's free and open during business hours.

profile_bio.description: Started/sold some startups. Founder + president of Hampton @hamptonfounders. Host My First Million podcast on the side.

text:
George Washington is one of my favorite presidents to read about because he was nearly a perfect leader and an amazing leader.

He was also nearly incorruptible.

When leading the army during the revolution, he tried to quit not once, but twice as he said he didn't think he was good enough to get the job done.

“I am fully sensible of the many difficulties under which I labor, and of my own deficiencies. I cannot promise myself success.”

Yet, he was exactly what we needed. The war likely could not have been won without him. 

He spent nearly 7 years away from home, fighting. Normally, generals always spent summers away.

Then when it came time to elect a our first president, he didn't even want to run.

Someone nominated him, he didn't run. Then he received every single electoral vote.

But after two terms, he retired, when there were no term limits. This shocked people because they wanted him to stay in power. 

He said: The power under the Constitution will always be in the people. It is entrusted for certain defined purposes, and for a certain limited period, to representatives of their own choosing.

That's a damn servant leader. A stark contrast to what we have now.

text:
I've got a buddy who sold his company for ~$70m.

So, it was a massive financial success. 

However, he HATED the life he made for himself while running it.

His company felt like a prison:

- 16-hour days. Skipped his honeymoon. 
- Micromanaged everything. 
- Built his entire identity around the selling.

After selling, like all of us idiots, he started his second company.

My buddy, btw, is @jnpayne.

But for his next company, he made a bunch of rules. Rules so that he'd be happier during the build process.

- The hours were 8 to 5. 
- No weekends. 
- Fully remote. 
- No meetings where he wasn't essential.
- And a month-long family trip to Europe every summer with his wife and three kids.

Recently, he did something super cool: moved his entire family of five to Spain.

Other things Josh did that I like:

- he removed himself from operational meetings
- scaled every 1:1 to monthly
- muted Slack channels where he wasn't essential
- assigned owners to projects and stopped poking around. 

His words: "I frankly just dropped the ball on a lot of things."

But...it seems to be working. 

His new company, its called Onward, and they're very profitable with 20 people.

I LOVE hearing stories like this.

Because the entire point of this business thing, at least for me, is to put your dent into the world but doing it your way.

On twitter, podcasts, etc...there are so many rules that we take for granted. So we often just fit into a box of business building. 

But I've done so many interviews with baller founders + see it in Hampton...there are 10,000's ways to kill it.

profile_bio.description: Started/sold some startups. Founder + president of Hampton @hamptonfounders. Host My First Million podcast on the side.

text:
I asked this question in Hampton's Slack: 

who here scaled to $20-$50m+ working 40 hours a week?

Replies (anonymized):

Founder A who crossed $50M:

"My secret was having 4 kids with intense schedules and a great management team. The family commitment is a great forcing function."

His 40 hours now are less chaotic than his old 60 ever were.

Founder B - crossed $20M, pacing $30M:

"95% of decisions don't matter, and virtually all can be reversed. Most founders who grind crazy hours associate identity and prestige with being involved in every decision.

Letting go of that need for control gave me back most of my time."

Founder C built two companies to $15-20M:

First one: worked like a dog. Most of the extra hours were learning and putting out fires he created.

Second one: under 40 hours once he had a focused strategy and competent leaders.

"It's your competency at the thing you're doing and the number of things you're doing."

Founder D - just crossed $20M:

Got 12 employees onto the cap table as small owners through an internal succession plan.

"Those employee-owners solve problems before they ever reach me. I'm down to 40 hours, but those hours are also much less chaotic than my previous 60."

Founder E - $20M+ agency:

Instead of chasing volume with small clients, they landed a few massive enterprise accounts. Same work unlocks 8 figures of spend.

Then added an exec layer to shift from client focus to business focus. Hours dropped every year.

Founder F - $20M+:

"Ruthless simplification. Every quarter we cut things."
Scariest move: firing their largest client. The client was consuming the entire team and was misaligned with what they were good at.

Revenue went up after.

Founder G:

"Hire exceptional people. Not every hire needs to be exceptional, but a few will make problems that would normally fall on you disappear.

Why do others fail? They tell themselves they've hired exceptional people but have never actually worked with one. Their standards are unknowingly low."

----

Anyone else done it? Wanna add anything?

text:
ANNOUNCEMENT. 

We’re opening up 3 new Hampton chapters.  

San Diego, Philly, DC.

But first, real quick:

Running an IRL network  has tons of cool but hard parts about it.

Cool parts:

It benefits our Atlanta chapter if we have a Hong Kong chapter. So when they travel there, they have their people or wanna connect to someone in HK, they have that. 

So, expanding is good.

But the hard part:

Its VERY EASY to grow these businesses. Just sell the dream. Easy. 

The hard part is doing actual vetting + making the Core so good a member wants to stay for 20+ years. Making sure you're getting the best people who fit our standards (tech, traction, character) you never sacrifice integrity for $$$.

For example, if we have a 6 person Core group in ATL and they need 2 more ballers...recruiting, vetting, training those new members to be great Core group members...while also having integrity and not coming off salesy/transactional...that's the f*cking hard part.

But thankfully we're figuring it out.

Which is why we're coming to philly, san diego, and DC.

So, get a damn IRL core group: 

- monthly irl meeting w 8 other founders like you (ave member does $20m/year)
- going through the same stuff
- calling you out
- helping you learn

We picked these cities because there's a huge, untapped startup community. 

So if want to be around other people building startups, apply. I review every app.

Apply: https://t.co/2SSMOf5dXt
